title: To W. A. - Or Ever The Knightly Years Were Gone
content:
Or ever the knightly years were gone
    With the old world to the grave,
    I was a King in Babylon
    And you were a Christian Slave.

    I saw, I took, I cast you by,
    I bent and broke your pride.
    You loved me well, or I heard them lie,
    But your longing was denied.
    Surely I knew that by and by
    You cursed your gods and died.

    And a myriad suns have set and shone
    Since then upon the grave
    Decreed by the King in Babylon
    To her that had been his Slave.

    The pride I trampled is now my scathe,
    For it tramples me again.
    The old resentment lasts like death,
    For you love, yet you refrain.
    I break my heart on your hard unfaith,
    And I break my heart in vain.

    Yet not for an hour do I wish undone
    The deed beyond the grave,
    When I was a King in Babylon
    And you were a Virgin Slave.
meaning:
"To W. A. - Or Ever The Knightly Years Were Gone" is a quintessential example of William Ernest Henley's signature style...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
